title: The Hunchback of Notre Dame by Victor Hugo

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Paris: The City of Notre Dame
informationalTexts.text: In the 1400s, Paris was very different from the city we know today. The streets were narrow and twisted, often muddy or filled with garbage. Buildings leaned close together, blocking out sunlight. Most people lived in small, cramped spaces. But rising above it all was Notre Dame Cathedral — a massive stone church that took nearly 200 years to build. With its twin towers, flying buttresses, and stained glass windows, Notre Dame was the most impressive building in Paris. It stood on an island in the middle of the Seine River. For the people of medieval Paris, the cathedral was the center of their world — a place for worship, festivals, and seeking sanctuary from danger.
questions: How long did it take to build Notre Dame Cathedral?
Where was Notre Dame located in Paris?
Why was Notre Dame so important to the people of medieval Paris?

informationalTexts.title: Victor Hugo: Voice of the People
informationalTexts.text: Victor Hugo was born in France in 1802. He became one of the most famous writers of the 1800s. Hugo believed that stories should speak for people who had no voice — the poor, the outcast, the misunderstood. He wrote The Hunchback of Notre Dame when he was only 29 years old. The book was published in 1831 and became an instant success. Hugo wanted to save Notre Dame Cathedral, which was falling into ruin at the time. His book made people care about the old cathedral again, and it was restored. Hugo also wrote Les Misérables, another famous story about outcasts and injustice. He lived to be 83 years old and is remembered as a champion of human rights.
questions: What did Victor Hugo believe stories should do?
Why did Hugo write The Hunchback of Notre Dame?
What happened to Notre Dame Cathedral after the book was published?

informationalTexts.title: Gothic Cathedrals: Building to the Sky
informationalTexts.text: Gothic cathedrals like Notre Dame were marvels of medieval engineering. They featured pointed arches, tall spires, and huge stained glass windows. Flying buttresses — stone supports on the outside — held up the walls so the inside could have massive windows instead of solid stone. Building a cathedral could take generations. Entire families of craftsmen — stonecutters, glassmakers, carpenters — worked on these buildings their whole lives. The cathedrals were designed to inspire awe. When people stepped inside, they looked up at ceilings that seemed to touch heaven. The colored light streaming through stained glass windows told Bible stories in pictures for people who couldn't read.
questions: What are flying buttresses and what do they do?
Why did it take so long to build gothic cathedrals?
How did stained glass windows help people learn Bible stories?

informationalTexts.title: The Life of a Bell-Ringer
informationalTexts.text: In medieval times, church bells were the timekeeper for the entire city. They marked the hours, called people to prayer, warned of danger, and announced celebrations. A bell-ringer lived in the church tower and rang the bells at precise times throughout the day and night. It was hard, lonely work. The bell-ringer had to climb hundreds of stairs many times a day. The sound of the bells could damage hearing over time. Bell-ringers often lived isolated lives, with the church as their entire world. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, Quasimodo is the bell-ringer of Notre Dame. The bells are his only friends, and he gives them all names.
questions: What purposes did church bells serve in medieval cities?
Why was being a bell-ringer hard work?
In the story, who is the bell-ringer of Notre Dame?

informationalTexts.title: Sanctuary: The Right to Seek Safety
informationalTexts.text: In medieval Europe, churches offered sanctuary — a legal right for people accused of crimes to seek safety inside. If someone fled to a church and claimed sanctuary, the law said they couldn't be arrested there. The sanctuary seeker had to stay inside the church grounds and couldn't leave, but they were protected from immediate punishment. This gave them time to make arrangements or prove their innocence. Church sanctuary was serious — even kings had to respect it, though they sometimes surrounded the church and waited for the person to come out. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, Notre Dame Cathedral provides sanctuary to a character in danger, and armed guards wait outside, unable to enter.
questions: What does 'sanctuary' mean?
What happened if someone claimed sanctuary in a church?
Could kings' soldiers enter a church to arrest someone in sanctuary?

informationalTexts.title: The Romani People: Outsiders in Medieval Europe
informationalTexts.text: The Romani people, often called 'gypsies' (a term now considered offensive), migrated to Europe from India hundreds of years ago. They traveled in groups, speaking their own language and practicing their own customs. Because they lived differently from settled Europeans, they faced prejudice and discrimination. Many Europeans feared and mistrusted them, falsely accusing them of being thieves and sorcerers. In reality, Romani people were skilled musicians, dancers, metalworkers, and horse traders. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, one of the main characters is a young Romani woman named Esmeralda. She faces cruel treatment because of prejudice against her people.
questions: Where did the Romani people originally come from?
Why did Romani people face discrimination in medieval Europe?
What skills did Romani people actually have?

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Justice: Harsh and Unfair
informationalTexts.text: Justice in medieval times was often brutal and unfair. Trials were short, with little evidence required for conviction. Poor people and outsiders received harsher punishments than nobles. Public executions and punishments were common — they were meant to frighten others into obeying the law. Torture was used to get confessions. Sometimes 'trials' weren't trials at all, but mob justice where crowds decided someone's fate. Women accused of witchcraft, Romani people, and anyone considered different faced extreme danger. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, several characters face unjust treatment from the legal system, showing how cruel medieval justice could be.
questions: What were medieval trials like?
Why were public punishments common?
Who was most likely to receive unfair treatment from medieval justice?

informationalTexts.title: Festivals in Medieval Paris
informationalTexts.text: Medieval people worked hard and their lives were often difficult, so festivals and celebrations were important breaks from daily struggle. The Festival of Fools was a wild celebration held each year in Paris. For one day, normal rules were turned upside down. People wore masks and costumes. A 'Pope of Fools' was elected — usually the person who could make the ugliest face. There was music, dancing, eating, and drinking in the streets. The festival gave ordinary people a chance to mock the powerful and laugh at serious things. The Hunchback of Notre Dame begins during the Festival of Fools, when Quasimodo is crowned Pope of Fools because of his unusual appearance.
questions: Why were festivals important to medieval people?
What happened during the Festival of Fools?
How was the 'Pope of Fools' chosen?

informationalTexts.title: Life in a Medieval City
informationalTexts.text: Medieval Paris was crowded, dirty, and dangerous. Most people lived in small wooden houses that were fire hazards. There was no running water — people got water from public wells or the river. Garbage and sewage were thrown into the streets. Diseases spread easily. The streets belonged to whoever was strongest — thieves and gangs controlled many areas. At night, there was no street lighting, so the city was pitch black and even more dangerous. Rich people traveled with armed guards. The Church and its buildings, like Notre Dame, were islands of relative safety and order in a chaotic city. The contrast between the beauty of the cathedral and the harsh reality of the streets around it is an important part of Hugo's story.
questions: Where did medieval people get their water?
Why was nighttime especially dangerous in medieval Paris?
What made Notre Dame different from the rest of the city?

informationalTexts.title: Beauty and Ugliness: Medieval Views
informationalTexts.text: In medieval times, many people believed that outer appearance reflected inner character. If someone was beautiful, people assumed they were good. If someone looked different or deformed, people often feared them and thought they were evil or cursed by God. This belief was deeply unfair but widely held. People with disabilities were often hidden away or treated cruelly. They had no protections and little hope for kindness. Victor Hugo challenged this belief in The Hunchback of Notre Dame. His main character, Quasimodo, is considered ugly by everyone — but Hugo shows that he has a good, loyal, loving heart. Meanwhile, other characters who are beautiful on the outside reveal themselves to be cruel and selfish.
questions: What did many medieval people believe about appearance and character?
How were people with disabilities treated in medieval times?
What message does Victor Hugo send about appearance in his story?

informationalTexts.title: Archdeacons and Church Power
informationalTexts.text: An archdeacon was a high-ranking church official, second only to the bishop. Archdeacons had great power — they controlled church property, managed other priests, and enforced church law. In medieval society, the Church was as powerful as kings. Church officials like archdeacons could make decisions that affected people's entire lives. They controlled education (most schools were run by the Church), had their own courts, and could excommunicate people (banish them from the Church), which was socially devastating. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, Claude Frollo is the archdeacon of Notre Dame. He has great power but struggles with his own inner conflicts between duty and desire.
questions: What position did an archdeacon hold in the Church?
What kinds of power did church officials have?
Who is the archdeacon in The Hunchback of Notre Dame?

informationalTexts.title: Love and Obsession: Crossing the Line
informationalTexts.text: Love is a powerful emotion that can make people do wonderful things — sacrifice for others, show courage, be generous. But when love becomes obsession, it turns destructive. Obsession is when you want something or someone so badly that you stop caring about what's right, what's fair, or what the other person wants. Obsessed people convince themselves that their feelings justify any action. They may hurt the person they claim to love. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, several characters love the same woman. Some love her truly and want her to be happy. Others are obsessed with her and want to possess her, even if it destroys her. Hugo shows the difference between real love and dangerous obsession.
questions: What's the difference between love and obsession?
How do obsessed people justify their actions?
What does Victor Hugo show about love in his story?

informationalTexts.title: Stained Glass: Stories in Light
informationalTexts.text: Stained glass windows were masterpieces of medieval art. Craftsmen cut pieces of colored glass and joined them with lead strips to create pictures. These windows told stories from the Bible and the lives of saints. When sunlight shone through them, the colored light filled the cathedral with glowing reds, blues, greens, and golds. It felt magical. The windows served a purpose beyond beauty — they were books for people who couldn't read. A person could look at the windows and see stories of creation, miracles, heroes, and moral lessons. Creating stained glass required incredible skill. The techniques were closely guarded secrets passed from master to apprentice.
questions: How were stained glass windows made?
What stories did stained glass windows tell?
Why were these windows important for people who couldn't read?

informationalTexts.title: Gargoyles: Guardians and Waterspouts
informationalTexts.text: Gargoyles are the strange, monster-like stone figures you see on gothic cathedrals. They look scary — like dragons, demons, or twisted human faces. But they served a practical purpose: gargoyles were waterspouts. Rain collected on the cathedral's roof and flowed out through the gargoyles' mouths, keeping water away from the building's walls and foundation. The scary designs were meant to frighten away evil spirits. Craftsmen let their imaginations run wild creating these creatures. Each gargoyle was unique. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, Quasimodo spends much of his time among the gargoyles on Notre Dame's towers. He feels more comfortable with these stone monsters than with human beings.
questions: What practical purpose did gargoyles serve?
Why were gargoyles designed to look scary?
Where does Quasimodo spend his time in the story?

informationalTexts.title: Loyalty: Standing by Someone No Matter What
informationalTexts.text: Loyalty means staying true to someone even when it's difficult or dangerous. A loyal person doesn't abandon their friends when things go wrong. They don't betray secrets. They stand by people they care about even when others turn away. Loyalty is tested most strongly when being loyal costs you something. It's easy to be loyal when it's convenient. True loyalty appears when you have to choose between your friend and your own safety, comfort, or social standing. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, Quasimodo shows extraordinary loyalty to people who show him even small kindnesses. His loyalty is pure and unconditional — he never asks what he'll get in return.
questions: What does loyalty mean?
When is loyalty tested most strongly?
What kind of loyalty does Quasimodo show?

informationalTexts.title: The Power of Mobs
informationalTexts.text: A mob is a large crowd of people acting together emotionally rather than rationally. When people gather in an angry or fearful crowd, they often do things they would never do alone. Mob mentality is dangerous because individual judgment disappears. People feel anonymous in a crowd and feel less responsible for their actions. Mobs feed on emotion — anger, fear, excitement — and can quickly turn violent. In medieval times, mobs sometimes attacked people they were told to fear — accused witches, religious minorities, anyone different. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, mobs play a powerful and dangerous role at several key moments. Hugo shows how easily fear can turn a crowd into something destructive.
questions: What is mob mentality?
Why do people in mobs act differently than they would alone?
What role do mobs play in The Hunchback of Notre Dame?

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Clothing: What You Wore Told Who You Were
informationalTexts.text: In medieval times, your clothing immediately told everyone your social class. Laws even regulated what people could wear! Nobles wore silk, velvet, and fur in bright colors like red, purple, and gold. They had jeweled belts, fancy hats, and embroidered cloaks. Merchants and craftsmen wore wool in darker colors. Poor people wore rough brown or gray cloth — often the same tunic day after day. Romani people wore colorful, flowing clothes that made them stand out. Priests wore black robes. You could look at someone and instantly know if they were rich or poor, powerful or powerless. Dressing 'above your station' could get you in trouble. In the story, Esmeralda's colorful dancer's clothes mark her as an outsider.
questions: How did clothing show social class in medieval times?
What colors and fabrics did nobles wear?
What kind of clothing did Esmeralda wear?

informationalTexts.title: Witchcraft Accusations: Dangerous Superstitions
informationalTexts.text: In medieval Europe, many people believed in witchcraft and magic. Women who were different — too beautiful, too smart, too independent — were sometimes accused of being witches. Romani women faced especially high risk of these accusations. The 'evidence' for witchcraft was absurd by today's standards: owning a black cat, knowing herbal medicine, dancing too well, or simply being disliked. Being accused of witchcraft was extremely dangerous. Torture was used to force confessions. Trials were unfair. Punishments were severe — often death. Thousands of innocent people died due to these accusations. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, Esmeralda is accused of witchcraft simply because she's a Romani woman who dances beautifully and has a trained goat.
questions: What kind of 'evidence' was used to accuse someone of witchcraft?
Who faced the most danger of being accused?
Why is Esmeralda accused of witchcraft?

informationalTexts.title: Courage: Doing What's Right Despite Fear
informationalTexts.text: Courage doesn't mean not being afraid. Courage means doing the right thing even when you're terrified. It means standing up for someone when everyone else turns away. It means risking your own safety to help another person. Physical courage (facing danger) is one type. Moral courage (standing up for what's right even when it's unpopular) is another type, often harder. In medieval times, showing courage by protecting an outcast or speaking against injustice could cost you everything. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, several characters show different types of courage. Quasimodo shows physical courage by protecting Esmeralda. Other characters fail to show moral courage when it matters most.
questions: What's the difference between physical courage and moral courage?
Why is doing something despite fear courageous?
What type of courage does Quasimodo show?

informationalTexts.title: The Seine River: Lifeblood of Paris
informationalTexts.text: The Seine River flows through the heart of Paris. In medieval times, it was essential to the city's survival. Boats brought food, wine, wood, and other goods to Paris via the river. Fishermen caught fish from it. People (who didn't care about cleanliness) even got drinking water from it. Notre Dame Cathedral stands on Île de la Cité, an island in the middle of the Seine. This location was strategic — the island was easier to defend than the mainland. The river also served as a boundary and an escape route. Bridges connected the island to both banks. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, the Seine and the island setting create natural barriers during the story's dramatic final scenes.
questions: What purposes did the Seine River serve for medieval Paris?
Where does Notre Dame Cathedral stand in relation to the river?
Why was the island location strategic?

informationalTexts.title: Social Class: Rigid and Unfair
informationalTexts.text: Medieval society was divided into strict social classes that were nearly impossible to escape. At the top were kings and nobles. Below them were clergy (church officials). Next came merchants and craftsmen. At the bottom were peasants and laborers. Outside this system entirely were outcasts — Romani people, people with disabilities, criminals, and anyone who didn't fit in. Your birth determined your future. A peasant's child would be a peasant. A noble's child would be noble. There was almost no way to move up. The system was maintained by force and by convincing people it was God's will. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, characters suffer because of where society has placed them. Hugo questions whether this system is fair or just.
questions: What were the main social classes in medieval society?
Could people move from one class to another?
Who were considered outcasts in medieval society?

informationalTexts.title: Redemption: Finding Goodness After Mistakes
informationalTexts.text: Redemption means making up for your mistakes and wrong actions by doing good. It means recognizing you've hurt someone and trying to make it right. People who seek redemption don't make excuses — they take responsibility and change their behavior. Redemption is difficult because it requires admitting you were wrong, which hurts your pride. It requires changing, which is hard work. Not everyone is capable of redemption. Some people refuse to admit their mistakes. Others admit them but won't change. True redemption requires both recognition and action. In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, some characters find redemption through sacrifice and love. Others remain trapped by their pride and obsessions.
questions: What does redemption mean?
Why is redemption difficult?
What does true redemption require?

informationalTexts.title: Architecture as Character
informationalTexts.text: In The Hunchback of Notre Dame, the cathedral itself is almost a character in the story. Victor Hugo describes Notre Dame in loving detail — its towers, bells, hidden passages, and stone carvings. The cathedral has stood for centuries and will stand for centuries more. It represents permanence while human lives are brief. It provides sanctuary while the streets outside are dangerous. It rises toward heaven while human conflicts rage below. Hugo was passionate about saving old buildings. He believed architecture tells the story of civilization. When he wrote this novel, Notre Dame was crumbling and neglected. His book made people care about the cathedral again and inspired its restoration. The building was saved because Hugo showed how much it mattered.
questions: How does Hugo treat Notre Dame Cathedral in his story?
What does the cathedral represent?
How did Hugo's book help save Notre Dame?

informationalTexts.title: Tragedy: When Good People Suffer
informationalTexts.text: A tragedy is a story where things end badly despite the characters' best efforts. In a tragedy, good people suffer, sometimes because of their own flaws but often because the world is unfair. Tragic stories don't have happy endings. They show that life isn't always fair, that love doesn't always triumph, and that courage sometimes isn't enough. Why write tragedies? Because they show us truth. Not everything ends well. Sometimes heroes fail. Sometimes the innocent are hurt. Tragedies make us think about justice, about how society treats people, about what matters. The Hunchback of Notre Dame is a tragedy. Hugo doesn't give us a comfortable ending. Instead, he makes us face uncomfortable truths about prejudice, cruelty, and how society treats people who are different.
questions: What is a tragedy in literature?
Why do authors write tragedies instead of happy stories?
Why is The Hunchback of Notre Dame considered a tragedy?

grammar.lesson: Compound sentences join two independent clauses with a coordinating conjunction (for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so). Example: 'Quasimodo rang the bells, and the sound echoed across Paris.' Practice combining simple sentences.

Collective nouns (crowd, mob, group, family) can be singular or plural depending on meaning. Example: 'The crowd was angry' (acting as one) vs. 'The crowd were arguing among themselves' (individuals). Practice choosing correct verbs.

Adjective clauses add information about nouns using who, whom, whose, which, that. Example: 'The cathedral, which stood for centuries, was the heart of Paris.' Practice identifying and creating adjective clauses.

Use commas after introductory words, phrases, or clauses. Example: 'Despite the danger, Quasimodo protected her.' 'When the mob arrived, the guards blocked the door.' Practice adding commas correctly.
Pronouns must agree with their antecedents in number and gender. Example: 'Each bell has its own sound' (singular). 'The bells have their own names' (plural). Practice matching pronouns correctly.
An appositive renames or explains a noun and is set off by commas. Example: 'Claude Frollo, the archdeacon, had great power.' Practice identifying and punctuating appositives.
Use quotation marks around spoken words. Punctuation goes inside closing quotes. Example: 'Help me,' she whispered. Practice punctuating dialogue correctly with proper comma and period placement.
Form future tense with 'will' + verb. Example: 'He will protect her.' 'They will attack at dawn.' Some verbs use 'going to' for future: 'I am going to help.' Practice using future tense.
Comparative compares two things (add -er or use 'more'). Superlative compares three+ (add -est or use 'most'). Example: 'kinder, kindest' or 'more beautiful, most beautiful.' Practice correct forms.

Good writers vary sentence structure. Use simple, compound, and complex sentences. Start some with adverbs, others with phrases. Example: mix 'He climbed the tower' with 'Although exhausted, he climbed the tower.' Practice revision for variety.

Conjunctive adverbs (however, therefore, meanwhile, moreover) connect ideas between sentences or clauses. Use with semicolon: 'He loved her; however, she did not return his feelings.' Practice using these connectors.

Capitalize names of people, places, and specific things. Example: 'Notre Dame Cathedral,' 'the Seine River,' 'Archdeacon Frollo.' Capitalize titles before names but not after: 'Captain Phoebus' but 'Phoebus, the captain.' Practice correct capitalization.

language.lesson: arch = ruler/chief (archdeacon, monarch), bell = war or beautiful (bellicose, belle), spect = look (spectacle, inspect). Knowing roots helps unlock word meanings. Find words with these roots in sentences.
Authors give examples to clarify meaning. Example: 'The cathedral's features — its towers, bells, and stained glass — inspired awe.' The examples define 'features.' Practice using example clues.
mis- = wrong (mistrust, misjudge), pre- = before (prejudice, predict), re- = again (restore, return). Add these prefixes to base words and explain how meaning changes.
Personification gives human qualities to non-human things. Example: 'The cathedral watched over the city.' 'The bells sang their songs.' Create personification for objects in the story.

Synonyms have similar meanings but different feelings. 'Different' is neutral. 'Unique' is positive. 'Weird' or 'strange' can be negative. Choose synonyms carefully based on the tone you want. Practice word choice.
consequence = result of action, perspective = point of view, impact = strong effect. These words help discuss literature and ideas. Use them in sentences about the story.
-ity = state of being (loyalty, brutality), -ous = full of (monstrous, courageous), -able = capable of (lovable, forgivable). Add suffixes to base words and use new words in context.

Idioms mean something different from literal words. 'Turn a blind eye' = ignore. 'Throw stones' = judge others. 'Beauty is in the eye of the beholder' = people see beauty differently. Explain idioms' real meanings.

language.topic: Greek and Latin Roots: mal, bene, mob
language.lesson: mal = bad (malice, malformed), bene = good (benevolent, benefit), mob = move (mobile, immobile). Use these roots to figure out unfamiliar words. Practice with word lists.

Some words have general and specific meanings. 'Arch' can mean curved structure or chief (as in archdeacon). 'Sanctuary' means holy place or safe refuge. 'Column' means support pillar or newspaper section. Practice using context.

Understanding opposites helps clarify meaning. Beauty ↔ ugliness, justice ↔ injustice, loyalty ↔ betrayal, mercy ↔ cruelty. The story explores these contrasts. Identify antonym pairs in text and explain their importance.

Literary terms help discuss stories. Tragedy = sad story, protagonist = main character, antagonist = opposing force, conflict = struggle, theme = main idea. Use these terms to analyze The Hunchback of Notre Dame.

writing.prompt: Opinion: Do you think buildings from the past should be preserved and restored, or should old buildings be torn down to make room for new ones? Give at least three reasons. (4-5 paragraphs)
Narrative: Imagine you're a craftsman working on building Notre Dame Cathedral. Write about a day in your life — what you do, what you see, how you feel about the work. (4-5 paragraphs)
Informational: Explain what sanctuary meant in medieval times and why it was important. Use facts from the informational text and give examples. (3-4 paragraphs)
Opinion: Is it ever okay to break the law to protect someone who is being treated unfairly? Explain your answer with reasons and examples. (4-5 paragraphs)
Narrative: Write a story set in medieval Paris. Include sensory details (sights, sounds, smells) and dialogue. Show what daily life was like. (4-5 paragraphs)
Informational: Compare and contrast how people with disabilities are treated in modern times versus medieval times. Include at least three differences. (3-4 paragraphs)
Opinion: Victor Hugo believed that outer appearance doesn't tell you about inner character. Do you agree or disagree? Support your position with examples. (4-5 paragraphs)
Narrative: Write about a time you showed loyalty to a friend, or a time someone showed loyalty to you. Include what happened and why it mattered. (4-5 paragraphs)
Informational: Explain how clothing showed social class in medieval times. Describe what different groups wore and why this system was unfair. (3-4 paragraphs)
Opinion: Which type of courage is harder: physical courage (facing physical danger) or moral courage (standing up for what's right)? Explain your reasoning. (4-5 paragraphs)
Narrative: Imagine you could move between social classes in medieval Paris. Write a story about spending one day as a noble and one day as a peasant. What do you notice? (4-5 paragraphs)
Informational: Explain why Victor Hugo wrote The Hunchback of Notre Dame and what impact his book had. Include how art and stories can change the world. (3-4 paragraphs)

journal.prompt: Victor Hugo believed stories should give voice to people who are overlooked or mistreated. If you could tell a story about someone who needs a voice, who would it be and why? (1-2 paragraphs)
Quasimodo lived a lonely life in the bell tower. Write about a time you felt lonely or left out. How did it feel? What would have helped? (1-2 paragraphs)
The Romani people faced prejudice because they were different. Have you ever witnessed someone being treated unfairly because they're different? How did it make you feel? (1-2 paragraphs)
The Festival of Fools was a time when normal rules were turned upside down. If you could create a festival where rules were different for a day, what would it be like? (1-2 paragraphs)
Medieval people wrongly judged others by appearance. Describe a time when someone's appearance surprised you — they were kinder, smarter, or braver than you expected. (1-2 paragraphs)
The story explores the difference between love and obsession. What do you think makes a relationship healthy versus unhealthy? (1-2 paragraphs)
Quasimodo felt more comfortable with stone gargoyles than with people. Is there a place where you feel especially comfortable and safe? Describe it. (1-2 paragraphs)
Mobs can make people do things they wouldn't do alone. Have you ever felt pressure from a group to do something you weren't sure about? What happened? (1-2 paragraphs)
In the story, people were accused of witchcraft based on no real evidence. Why do you think people sometimes believe accusations without proof? (1-2 paragraphs)
Notre Dame stands on an island, separated from the chaos of the city. If you could create your own safe island, what would it be like? (1-2 paragraphs)
Redemption means making up for your mistakes. Write about a time you made a mistake and tried to make it right. What did you learn? (1-2 paragraphs)
The Hunchback of Notre Dame is a tragedy — it doesn't have a happy ending. Why do you think authors sometimes write sad stories? What can we learn from them? (1-2 paragraphs)
